Many people believe that the Electoral College should be kept, and that doing away with it would ignore the will of the people. However, the Electoral College does many things, and the winner take all method of voting is not always an accurate reflection of the people's will.
First, the Electoral College is a compromise between those who wanted Congress to choose the president and those who wanted the people to elect him. It is an attempt to find a middle ground between State's rights and people's votes. Many people also argue that the Electoral College has helped to prevent electors from swing states from being overly important. Rawi Abdelal states, "The Electoral College is a fine-grained device that moderates the impact of swing states." This shows that the Electoral College is an essential part of our democracy and helps to prevent any state from having too much power.
Another reason to keep the Electoral College is that it is part of our country's history and institutions. The Electoral College is part of the United States constitution and has been used for every presidential election for over two centuries. It is a central part of our political system and changing it would be a significant change that could cause chaos and confusion. As the text of the same name states, "The Electoral College is part of our political DNA," and changing it would be going against what our country was founded on.
On the other hand, many people argue that the Electoral College is unfair and that we should change to election by popular vote. They say that the Electoral College often means that the candidate who does not win the popular vote will win the presidency. For example, in 2000 Al Gore recieved nearly 300,000 more popular votes than George W. Bush but still did not win the presidency. Changing to popular vote would help to avoid this problem and give the people a more accurate say in who wins the presidency.

It maintains the balance between the states and the people, and it is a part of our country's history and institutions. When people ask for advice, they sometimes talk to more than one person because seeking multiple opinions can help someone make a better choice. I think this is because having multiple perspectives can help identify potential flaws or biases that may have been overlooked in a single opinion. For example, if someone is trying to decide whether to take a new job, they may talk to friends, family, and a professional counselor. The friends and family may have a more emotional response to the decision, while the counselor may be able to provide more objective information about the job market. By considering all of these opinions, the person can make a more informed decision.

Another reason seeking multiple opinions can help is that it allows someone to consider different viewpoints. For instance, if someone is trying to decide whether to buy a new car, they may talk to a salesperson, a mechanic, and a financial advisor. The salesperson may be trying to sell the car, the mechanic may be able to identify any potential problems, and the financial advisor may be able to provide information about the long-term costs of owning the car. By hearing these different viewpoints, someone can make a more well-rounded decision.
Overall, seeking multiple opinions can help someone make a better choice because it allows them to consider a wider range of perspectives and potential flaws. By talking to multiple people, someone can make a more informed and thoughtful decision.

You cannot convince me, but I will allow you to try. In the article, "Unmasking the Face on Mars," NASA scientists have proven that the Face is not a natural landform. They have presented evidence that the Face is a mesa, a type of landform created by the erosion of Martian bedrock. The Face is located on the Cydonia region of Mars and is only 600 meters long and 80 meters wide.

These NASA scientists say that if the Face was a natural landform, it should have eroded away by now, but it has not. The Face is only a few million years old and was most likely formed by the same process that created other Martian mesas. Furthermore, the Face is furrowed in the middle and has a depression on top. The scientists say that the face is just a natural landform!
The evidence that the Face is a natural landform is overwhelming. There is no evidence, according to the article, that it was created by aliens or other artificial means. All the evidence points to the Face being a natural landform. So, while you can try to concoct an argument against it, I will be sticking to the truth! This is just not the Face on Mars in the first place.
The Face is just a natural landform.

Dear senator, The Electoral College is unfair, outdated, and irrational as Bradford Plumber stated. First of all, The electoral college consists of the electors, the meeting of the electors where they vote for President and Vise President, and the counting of the electoral votes by Congress. We should abolish the electoral college because it is unfair to voters. It's all because of the winnertakeall system that in each state, candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning. They focus on the tight races in the "swing" states. For example, during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all 25 of the largest media markets didn't get to see a single campaign ad.

One of the best arguments against the electoral college is the disaster factor. The state legislators are technically responsible for picking electors, and that those electors could always defy the will of the people. In 1960, segragationists in the Louisiana legislature nearly succeeded in replacing the Democratic electors with new electors who would oppose John F. Kennedy. This means that a popular vote for Kennedy would not have actually gone to him.

Another issue that most people worry about is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote. If that happens, the election would be thrown to the House of Representatives and the Senate would choose Vise President.

To say the least, abolishing the electoral college is the best idea. According to a gallop poll in 2000, I am not alone. The gallop poll was taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency. Over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now. How does the electoral college even have any defenderes left? Even in a close election in which the popular vote winner could lose the presidency.

The electoral college is widely regarded as an anachronism and I couldn't agree more. It is a thing from the past and should stay that way. The electoral college is not democratic in a modern sense because it is the electors who elect the president, not the people. Richard A. Posner even says that when you vote for a presidential candidate, you're actually voting for a slate of electors.
